title: Jocelyn Cramer
text: I am a current master’s student in Watershed Science at Colorado State University (CSU) based in Fort Collins, Colorado, with a focus on snow processes and forest management in mountain environments. My work explores how forest structure and management decisions influence snow accumulation, melt, and downstream water resources. I’m passionate about applying field measurements, data analysis, and applied science to support resilient forest and water management in a changing climate. My research plan is to complete my M.S. at CSU in the summer of 2026 and continue onto my Ph.D. in fall of 2026, pursuing a similar topic.

title: Current Research
text:
Snow Density Variability in Lodgepole Pine (Pinus contorta) Forests

Objectives

Quantify the spatial variability of snow density in lodgepole pine forests.
Examine correlation between snow density, snow depth, and forest structural characteristics.
Integrate drone-based LiDAR with empirical models to generate spatial predictions of snow depth and density.
Assess the implications of observed variability for snowpack estimation, hydrologic modeling, and forest management.



Study Area



Study area map


Figure 1. P. contorta is the primary tree species present in this area of Northern Colorado. Drone-based LiDAR flights were conducted in State Forest State Park (SFSP) as well as comparisons to nearby Rawah SNOTEL station (right). Study sites located in a similar aged stand were visited along Highway 14 (left).


Methods

Selected 14 upper montane P. contorta forest sites along Highway 14 in northern Colorado using randomly generated coordinates and conducted snowpack sampling during winter 2025.
Measured snow depth, snow density, and snow water equivalent (SWE) around 74 sampled trees using snow probes, snow corers, GPS. In the summer forest structure measurements were collected including tree height, DBH, canopy cover, and tree spacing.
Developed boosted regression tree (BRT) models in R to evaluate how forest structure, canopy conditions, and spatial position influenced snow depth and snow density using cross-validation and predictor importance metrics.
Processed airborne LiDAR snow-on and snow-off datasets to derive terrain and vegetation rasters, then applied BRT models to generate spatial predictions of snow depth and density across study sites.



Results

Figure 2. Snow density (kg/m3) and snow depth (m) measurements taken at the same locations. Early and late season regression lines illustrate seasonal separation in the depth–density relationship. Together the correlation is somewhat strong (R2 = 0.68). Separated by season timing the correlation strengthened (late season R2 = 0.92, and late season R2 = 0.87).

Figure 3. Snow water equivalent (SWE) measurements in millimeters (mm) at the Rawah SNOTEL station (blue line), compared to observed measurements along Highway 14. The observed measurements were separated by averaging all values or averaging only midpoint values for a given day. Midpoint measurements are closer to the SNOTEL values compared to all measurements.

Figure 4. (Left) Snow depth (m) measurements with average mean and standard deviation values for all data. (Right) SWE (mm) was also evaluated as average values with standard deviations for each measurement distance. All values were measured at 30 centimeter intervals. There is a clear difference between snow depth and density values within less than a meter compared to a meter away from the tree bole

Figure 5. Forest structure measurements showed strong correlations between tree height and diameter at breast height (DBH), while canopy cover, tree spacing, and distance to clearings reflected dense, relatively even-aged P. contorta stands.

Binary regression tree models identified canopy cover and measurement direction as the most influential controls on snow depth and snow density, with additional effects from DBH, neighboring trees, and proximity to forest clearings.


Figure 6. Snow Depth derived from a drone-based LiDAR flight within State Forest State Park.

LiDAR data shows inability to penetrate below the canopy, inhibiting the ability to measure snow depth and therefore requiring interpolation or modeling


Figure 7. BRT Modeled snow depth (m) allows for predictions beneath the canopy where LiDAR cannot penetrate.


Discussion + Conclusion
These are preliminary results for the completion of my master’s in Watershed Science Thesis Dissertation fulfillment at Colorado State University. The Discussion and Conclusion portions of this website cannot be completed due to unfinished data processing. This research and full webpage are estimated to be completed by July 2026.

title: Projects_Publications
text:
Current Research Projects
Snow Density Variability in Lodgepole Pine (Pinus contorta) Forests

Study focus: how snow distribution varies within forested stands relating to forest management
Location: montane watersheds in northern Colorado
Methods: field snow measurements, forest metrics, and data analysis
Relevance: implications for water supply and forest management under climate change



Ongoing & Past Projects
Human Scent Movement in Avalanche Debris for Search and Rescue Dogs

Evaluate how carbon dioxide (CO2) moves within recent avalanche sites as a proxy for human scent
Focus on in situ data collection in southcentral Alaska
